era_context: The year 518 CE marks the accession of Emperor Justin I to the Eastern Roman throne, a pivotal moment in early 6th-century history. This period encompasses the late Ostrogothic Kingdom in Italy, the Merovingian Frankish kingdoms in Western Europe, the Liang Dynasty in southern China, and the Sasanid Empire under Kavad I. Religious ferment, military competition, and the consolidation of early Christian monasticism characterize this era.

events.title: Justin I accedes to Eastern Roman throne
events.description: Justin I, a military officer of Illyrian origin, ascended to the Eastern Roman throne following the death of Emperor Anastasius I. Justin's accession marked a significant shift in imperial policy, particularly regarding religious orthodoxy (Justin favoured Chalcedonian Christianity over the Monophysitism of his predecessor) and relations with the Western Roman Church and the Papacy. His reign (518-527 CE) would set the stage for his successor Justinian I's ambitious reconquest of the Western Roman Empire. Justin's accession was facilitated by the military and the Senate, reflecting the complex power dynamics of the Eastern Roman state.
key_figures: Justin I (Eastern Roman Emperor, r. 518-527 CE)

sources.name: Procopius, History of the Wars and Secret History (written c. 550 CE)
Theodoret of Cyrrhus, Ecclesiastical History (written c. 440s CE, but covers this period)
Evagrius Scholasticus, Ecclesiastical History (written c. 590 CE)

events.certainty_note: Justin I's accession in 518 CE is one of the best-documented events of this period, attested in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: Leads to Justinian I's reign (527-565 CE) and reconquest of the Western Roman Empire; relates to broader religious and political transformations of the 6th century

events.title: Vitalian's rebellion against Anastasius I continues and intensifies
events.description: Vitalian, a military commander and Chalcedonian Christian zealot, led a major rebellion against Emperor Anastasius I, ostensibly over religious orthodoxy. The rebellion, which had begun in 513 CE, continued to pose a significant military and political threat to the empire in 518 CE. Vitalian's forces controlled much of the Balkans and Thrace, and he posed a serious challenge to imperial authority. The rebellion would continue into Justin I's reign and be finally suppressed in 519-520 CE.

sources.name: Procopius, History of the Wars (written c. 550 CE)
Evagrius Scholasticus, Ecclesiastical History (written c. 590 CE)

events.certainty_note: Vitalian's rebellion is well-documented in multiple sources. The rebellion began in 513 CE and continued through 518 CE, with the final suppression occurring in 519-520 CE.
cross_references: Part of broader religious and military conflicts in early 6th-century Eastern Roman Empire; relates to Nika Riots (532 CE)

events.title: Theodoric the Ostrogoth consolidates control of Italy
events.description: Theodoric the Ostrogoth (r. 493-526 CE) continued to consolidate his control over Italy in 518 CE, ruling as king of the Ostrogoths and nominally under the authority of the Eastern Roman Emperor. Theodoric's reign was marked by relative stability and cultural synthesis between Ostrogothic and Roman traditions. However, tensions with the Eastern Roman Empire were increasing, particularly as Justin I's accession signalled a shift in imperial policy toward greater intervention in Italian affairs. Theodoric's position would become increasingly precarious in the following years.
key_figures: Theodoric the Ostrogoth (King of the Ostrogoths, r. 493-526 CE)
Emperor Justin I (newly ascended Eastern Roman Emperor)

sources.name: Procopius, History of the Wars (written c. 550 CE)
Cassiodorus, Variae (administrative letters, compiled early 6th century)
Ennodius, Life of Epiphanius (written early 6th century)

events.certainty_note: Theodoric's rule in 518 CE is well-documented in administrative records (Cassiodorus's Variae) and later historical accounts. The year falls within the well-attested period of his reign.
cross_references: Leads to Boethius's execution (520 CE) and Justinian's reconquest of Italy (535-554 CE)

events.title: Liang Dynasty continues Buddhist patronage and cultural flourishing
events.description: Emperor Wu of Liang (r. 502-549 CE) continued his vigorous patronage of Buddhism, commissioning temple construction and supporting the translation of Buddhist texts. The year 518 CE falls within the period of intensive Buddhist cultural and intellectual activity in southern China. The Liang court was a major centre of Buddhist learning, attracting monks and scholars from across Asia.

sources.name: Liang Shu (History of the Liang Dynasty, compiled 7th century)
Buddhist biographical texts and temple records
Archaeological evidence of temple construction and Buddhist art

events.certainty_note: Emperor Wu's Buddhist patronage is well-documented in Liang Shu and corroborated by archaeological evidence. The year 518 CE falls within the documented period of intensive patronage.
cross_references: Part of broader Buddhist flourishing in China (5th-6th centuries); relates to later Tang Dynasty Buddhist culture

disconfirming_evidence: Justin I's accession date of 518 CE is universally accepted in modern scholarship, but some older sources give slightly different dates for specific events within his reign. Vitalian's rebellion is well-documented, but the precise chronology of individual battles and negotiations is sometimes disputed. Theodoric's position in 518 CE is often portrayed as stable, but some scholars emphasize the growing tensions with the Eastern Roman Empire that would lead to conflict. The significance of Buddhist patronage in 518 CE specifically (rather than the broader period) is not precisely documented.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 518 CE is moderate to good, particularly for Eastern Roman political and military events. The accession of Justin I is one of the best-documented events of the early 6th century. Ostrogothic sources (Cassiodorus) are relatively abundant but reflect the court's perspective. Chinese sources are good for the Liang Dynasty but reflect court-centred perspectives. Sasanid sources are sparse. The record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, demographic, and technological changes are poorly documented. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of northern Europe are absent from surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Justin I's accession set the stage for his nephew Justinian I's accession and ambitious reconquest policies
Vitalian's rebellion continued from 513 CE and was finally suppressed in 519-520 CE
Theodoric's consolidation of power preceded the execution of Boethius in 520 CE
Justin I's accession and religious orthodoxy shift led to reconciliation of the Acacian Schism in 519 CE
